decision_simulation: The real historical moment: December 2001, Argentina. The government must decide whether to freeze bank withdrawals (the "Corralito") to prevent a total collapse of the banking system, as depositors rush to pull out their savings amid fears the peso's 1:1 peg to the US dollar was about to break. The outcome was genuinely uncertain: freezing withdrawals could either stabilize the banks or trigger the exact social explosion authorities feared.Player choice: Impose the Corralito freeze, or allow unrestricted withdrawals and risk a full bank run.Historical resolution: The government imposed the Corralito. It triggered the Cacerolazo protests and the fall of President Fernando de la Rúa's government within days, followed by the formal abandonment of the dollar peg in early 2002.

understand.economic_impact: Argentina's currency has been restructured or renamed at least eight times since 1826, including two separate 10,000:1 devaluations (1983 and 1992) that erased four zeros from the currency each time Wikipedia: Argentine peso argentino. The 1989 hyperinflation, reaching 3,000–5,000% annually, wiped out savings and wages in real time Los Angeles Times, 1989.
understand.political_impact: The 2001 Corralito banking freeze directly triggered the Cacerolazo protests and the collapse of President Fernando de la Rúa's government within days Wikipedia: December 2001 riots in Argentina. The 2022 banknote series' return to historical Argentine figures after a decade of fauna-only currency was itself a symbolic political statement about national identity 90 Líneas feature.
understand.why_it_still_matters: Imagine saving money for a year, and at the end of that year, discovering it can buy far less than when you started — not because you spent it, but because the money itself lost its value while just sitting there. That has happened to Argentina's currency again and again for nearly 200 years, so often that the government has had to rename the currency itself multiple times, sometimes crossing out zeros because the numbers got too big to write comfortably on a banknote. This matters because it shows something most people never have to think about: money only works if everyone trusts it will still be worth something tomorrow. When that trust breaks — through bad decisions, crises, or years of printing too much money — ordinary people's life savings can disappear without them doing anything wrong at all. Valentina's story matters because she proves you can survive that, over and over, and still get up the next morning.
